questions.statement: Akbar was 13 years old when he was proclaimed emperor after Humayun's death.
questions.explanation: Akbar was indeed only 13 years old when he was proclaimed emperor in 1556 CE, following the sudden death of his father Humayun.

questions.statement: Humayun died in battle against the Afghans in 1556 CE.
questions.explanation: Humayun did not die in battle. He died from an accidental fall from the stairs of his library in 1556 CE.

questions.statement: Bairam Khan was Humayun's faithful general who served as regent for young Akbar.
questions.explanation: Bairam Khan had been a loyal and faithful general of Humayun. After Humayun's death, he became the regent for the 13-year-old Akbar and managed the affairs of the empire.

questions.statement: Akbar dismissed Bairam Khan from his position at the age of 21.
questions.explanation: Akbar dismissed Bairam Khan at the age of 18 (in 1560 CE), not 21. Akbar wanted to take charge of the administration himself.

questions.statement: The Second Battle of Panipat was fought in 1556 CE and the Mughal forces defeated Hemu.
questions.explanation: The Second Battle of Panipat (1556 CE) was a decisive battle in which Akbar's forces, under the guidance of Bairam Khan, defeated Hemu and crushed the Afghan threat.

questions.statement: Akbar personally led the Mughal forces at the Battle of Haldighati in 1576 CE.
questions.explanation: Akbar did not personally lead the forces at the Battle of Haldighati. The Mughal forces were led by Raja Man Singh, a Rajput commander in Akbar's service.

questions.statement: Akbar married the daughter of the Rajput ruler Udai Singh of Mewar.
questions.explanation: Akbar married the daughter of the Raja of Jaipur (Amber), not Udai Singh's daughter. Udai Singh of Mewar actually refused to accept Mughal authority.

questions.statement: Chittor was defended by Jai Mal when Akbar laid siege to it.
questions.explanation: When Akbar besieged Chittor, it was defended by Jai Mal (not Rana Pratap, who fought at Haldighati). During the siege, the Rajput women performed jauhar.

questions.statement: Rana Pratap successfully recovered Chittor before his death.
questions.explanation: Rana Pratap was not able to recover Chittor before his death. Despite his lifelong resistance against the Mughals, Chittor remained under Mughal control.

questions.statement: Akbar's empire was divided into 15 provinces called subas.
questions.explanation: Akbar organized his empire into 15 subas (provinces) for efficient administration. Each suba was headed by a governor called a subedar.

questions.statement: The mansab (rank) granted by Akbar was hereditary and passed from father to son.
questions.explanation: The mansab was NOT hereditary. When a mansabdar died, his mansab and all associated privileges reverted to the king. The king could then reassign the mansab as he saw fit.

questions.statement: The wazir (or diwan) was the head of the revenue department, not the military.
questions.explanation: The wazir or diwan was indeed the head of the revenue department. The head of the military department was the mir bakshi.

questions.statement: Todar Mal introduced the land revenue system known as Bandobast, using bamboo jarib with iron rings for measurement.
questions.explanation: Todar Mal, one of Akbar's key ministers, introduced the Bandobast system of land revenue. He used bamboo jarib fitted with iron rings for accurate land measurement, replacing the older rope-based system that could stretch.

questions.statement: Under Akbar's revenue system, half of the average produce was taken as the king's share.
questions.explanation: The king's share was one-third of the average produce, not half. This was considered a fair amount and helped reduce the burden on peasants.

questions.statement: Akbar abolished the jizya, a tax that had been imposed on non-Muslims.
questions.explanation: Akbar abolished the jizya (a tax on non-Muslims) as part of his policy of religious tolerance. He also abolished the pilgrim tax to promote harmony among all religions.

questions.statement: The Ibadat Khana was built by Akbar in 1575 CE at Delhi.
questions.explanation: The Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) was built by Akbar in 1575 CE at Fatehpur Sikri, not Delhi. It was a hall where scholars of different religions gathered for discussions.

questions.statement: Raja Man Singh was one of the Hindu courtiers who accepted Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi.
questions.explanation: Raja Man Singh REFUSED to join Din-i-Ilahi, as did Raja Bhagwan Das. Birbal was the ONLY Hindu courtier who accepted Din-i-Ilahi.

questions.statement: Akbar was illiterate but maintained a library of 24,000 books.
questions.explanation: Despite being illiterate himself, Akbar was a great patron of learning and had a library containing 24,000 books. He had scholars read books aloud to him.

questions.statement: Faizi was the author of the Akbarnama, a detailed account of Akbar's reign.
questions.explanation: The Akbarnama was written by Abul Fazl, not Faizi. Faizi was Abul Fazl's brother and was a renowned poet at Akbar's court.

questions.statement: Persian was the official court language during Akbar's reign.
questions.explanation: Persian was indeed the official court language under Akbar's rule. It was the language used for all administrative and official purposes in the Mughal Empire.
